ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chili pepper food product class; no specific grilled green chilli entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers green chilli peppers as a raw commodity; no grilled preparation facet applied. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227318009 refers to chili pepper as a substance; no grilled preparation variant exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is 3 skewers of grilled green chilli, approximately 60 g total, as served at Chinese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Peppers, hot chili, green, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170931)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy data for raw green hot chilli peppers (FDC ID 170931). Grilling causes minor moisture loss; values are presented on a per-100 g cooked-weight basis with a modest upward adjustment for moisture loss (~10%) applied to energy and macros. Sodium reflects unseasoned grilling; no added salt assumed. This is the closest defensible approved-source match as HPB Singapore Foodo and FAO/INFOODS SEA do not carry a specific grilled green chilli entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grilled green chilli. Green chilli peppers are a non-starchy vegetable with very low available carbohydrate content. A GI of 32 is assigned by analogy with other low-GI non-starchy vegetables (e.g., capsicum/bell pepper GI ~15–40 range) as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 5.3) / 100 ≈ 1.7, where carbohydrate per 60 g serving ≈ 5.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~9 mg per 100 g) and high potassium (~340 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Grilled green chilli is very low in sodium and provides meaningful potassium, making it a favourable vegetable choice for individuals managing hypertension; no added-salt seasoning should be used during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~32) and low glycemic load per serving (~1.7)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low GI of approximately 32 and a glycemic load of only 1.7 per 60 g serving, grilled green chilli has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, and high vitamin C content (~242 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled green chilli is extremely low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, and its high vitamin C content supports vascular health; it is a heart-friendly vegetable side dish when prepared without added oils or salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Green Chilli (烤青辣椒) is a simple Chinese-style grilled vegetable dish consisting of whole fresh green chilli peppers threaded onto skewers and cooked over direct heat or a charcoal grill until lightly charred and softened. As served in Singapore Chinese restaurants such as Wan Jia Fu, the dish typically comes as three skewers (approximately 60 g total). The grilling process imparts a smoky, slightly charred flavour while mellowing the raw heat of the chilli, producing a tender texture with a mildly spicy, savoury taste. Green chilli peppers are nutritionally notable for their exceptionally high vitamin C content (approximately 242 mg per 100 g), which far exceeds the daily recommended intake in a single serving. They are very low in calories (~43 kcal per 100 g), low in fat, and provide modest dietary fibre and potassium. The glycemic index is low (~32), making this dish suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. The very low sodium content makes it appropriate for hypertension management. Clinically, grilled green chilli is a low-concern food for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease when prepared without added salt, oil, or high-sugar dipping sauces. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for grilled green chilli exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw green hot chilli pepper data (FDC ID 170931) with a minor adjustment for moisture loss during grilling. GI is estimated by analogy with comparable non-starchy vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170931 (raw green hot chilli peppers) with approximately 10% moisture-loss adjustment applied to reflect grilled weight basis. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as green chilli peppers are a plant food that naturally contains no vitamin B12 (naturally zero). Vitamin D is 0.0 as green chilli peppers contain no meaningful vitamin D (naturally zero). Cholesterol is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food (naturally zero). Trans fat is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are present (naturally zero). Monounsaturated fat is listed as 0.0 based on USDA data showing negligible MUFA in green chilli peppers. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy vegetable GI range from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Serving size of 60 g (3 skewers) is estimated from typical restaurant portion for this dish format. Sodium reflects unseasoned grilling; actual sodium may be higher if dipping sauces or seasonings are applied. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Mixed' (input) to 'Vegetables' as grilled green chilli is a vegetable dish. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore. monounsaturated_fat_g set to 0.0 based on USDA source data showing negligible MUFA content in green chilli peppers.
